does: This paper extends contrastive preference tuning from English to a multilingual setting by training on self-generated model responses ranked by a reward model. The method, called CroCo, uses a single reward model trained on English preferences applied across 14 languages of varying resource levels. The authors evaluate two models and find that cross-lingual contrastive preference tuning successfully improves performance in most languages without requiring language-specific preference annotations. The approach works in both monolingual and multilingual pairing configurations while mitigating catastrophic forgetting from supervised fine-tuning, with the important finding that on-policy data is necessary for gains—off-policy responses significantly reduce benefits and online preference optimization underperforms the offline variant.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in preference-based learning for language models, where contrastive objectives between model-generated responses improve downstream performance. It extends prior English-only contrastive preference tuning methods to the multilingual setting, connecting to broader research on cross-lingual transfer in NLP. The approach leverages multilingual base models and reward models that have emerged from work on alignment and preference learning. The paper addresses a key gap in scaling preference tuning beyond English by exploring whether English-trained reward models can effectively rank responses across typologically diverse languages, extending the line of work on transfer learning and few-shot generalization in multilingual models.
enables: This work opens directions for scaling preference tuning across underrepresented languages without costly annotation efforts, suggesting that English preference signals can substitute for language-specific preferences in many cases. The finding that on-policy data is critical points toward more efficient data collection strategies for preference learning in low-resource settings. Future work could investigate why off-policy variants fail and how to better leverage existing corpora, explore the linguistic properties determining transfer success, and extend the approach to even more language pairs. The method also enables research into understanding when preference alignment generalizes cross-linguistically and could inform broader multilingual instruction-tuning and alignment strategies that reduce dependence on per-language annotation.
